## Vulnerability 9 — `update-center.js` assigns full server response to `innerHTML` (HIGH)
**File:** `core/src/main/resources/hudson/model/UpdateCenter/update-center.js:24,36,42`
```javascript
div.innerHTML = responseText;          // full server response fragment
tcell.innerHTML = scell.innerHTML;     // copied from another element
scheduleDiv.innerHTML = div.lastElementChild.innerHTML;
```
The Update Center page polls `./body` and re-injects full table row HTML from the server. While this is authenticated (only admins reach this page), the pattern places implicit trust on the server-rendered HTML containing no XSS payload — any sanitization failure in the server's rendering of plugin names, versions, or update messages will immediately execute in the browser.",
  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "The reported sinks in `update-center.js` are real DOM HTML sinks, but this report is a false positive for CWE-079 in the audited code path. The fetched `./body` response is not arbitrary user-controlled HTML: it is a server-rendered Jelly fragment from `hudson/model/UpdateCenter/body.jelly`, which is declared with `escape-by-default='true'` and only includes per-job `row.jelly` / `status.jelly` templates that also use escaping. The main potentially attacker-influenced field on this page is the plugin display name (`UpdateSite.Plugin.title` / `name`), but that value flows through `InstallationJob.getDisplayName()` into `DownloadJob/row.jelly` as `${it.displayName}` under escape-by-default, so markup is HTML-escaped before it ever reaches `responseText`. Other dynamic fields are likewise escaped: connection-check messages use `<j:out>` and even call `Functions.xmlEscape(...)` in Java first; failure details render `${it.problemStackTrace}` in an escaped Jelly template. So the client-side `innerHTML` assignments only reinsert Jenkins-generated, already-escaped markup for the download-progress UI. In real-world terms, this code would only become exploitable if there were some separate server-side escaping bug in those Jelly templates or a custom overridden UpdateCenter/view; the stock path shown here does not itself create a practical XSS.",
  "FP_Reason": ["D1"],
  "evidence": "Source-to-sink path examined: `update-center.js` fetches `./body` and assigns it to `div.innerHTML`, later copying `scell.innerHTML` and `div.lastElementChild.innerHTML`. The response is produced by `core/src/main/resources/hudson/model/UpdateCenter/body.jelly`, which starts with `<?jelly escape-by-default='true'?>` and includes `<st:include it=\"${i}\" page=\"row.jelly\" />` for each job. `core/src/main/resources/hudson/model/UpdateCenter/DownloadJob/row.jelly` also has `escape-by-default='true'` and renders the plugin name as `${it.displayName}`. The underlying data path is `UpdateCenter.InstallationJob.getDisplayName() -> plugin.getDisplayName()` and `UpdateSite.Plugin.getDisplayName()` derives from `title` or `name` loaded from update-center JSON (`UpdateSite.java` lines around 1279-1281 and 1344-1354). Because the Jelly template is escape-by-default, a malicious plugin title would be emitted as escaped text, not executable HTML. Additional dynamic fields are also escaped: `ConnectionCheckJob/row.jelly` uses `<j:out value=\"${s}\"/>`, while `UpdateCenter.java` already wraps exception-derived values with `Functions.xmlEscape(...)` before adding them to `statuses` (around lines 1727 and 1749). Failure output is rendered in `DownloadJob/Failure/status.jelly`, also with `escape-by-default='true'`, as `${it.problemStackTrace}` inside `<pre>`. Permission-wise, this endpoint is protected by `UpdateCenter.getTarget()` calling `Jenkins.get().checkPermission(Jenkins.SYSTEM_READ)`, and `index.jelly` declares `permission=\"${app.SYSTEM_READ}\"`; the report's 'only admins' assumption is inaccurate, but the key point is that the rendered fragment is escaped.",
  "new_reason:": ""
}"
}
0: { "response": "{ "judgement": "FP", "analysis": "The report points to a real raw-HTML helper (`Util.wrapToErrorSpan`) and a potentially dangerous rendering pattern (``), but the reported Jenkins core dataflow is not attacker-controlled, so the CWE-079 claim is not exploitable in the real code path. For `ClockDifference.toHtml()`, the wrapped string comes from `ClockDifference.toString()`, which derives text only from the numeric `diff` field using `Util.getTimeSpanString(abs)` and fixed localization templates such as `{0} ahead` / `{0} behind`. That `diff` is computed from timestamps (`System.currentTimeMillis()`) in the controller/agent clock-difference logic, not from node names or arbitrary remote strings. For `SwapSpaceMonitor.toHtml()`, the wrapped value is only `Functions.humanReadableByteSize(long)`, which formats a numeric long plus a fixed unit string. So while the sink may accept raw HTML, the source identified by the report is wrong for the reachable paths in this project. This is therefore a false positive under B1: the selected source is not a real XSS source.", "FP_Reason": [ "B1" ], "evidence": "Sink path exists: `core/src/main/resources/hudson/node_monitors/ClockMonitor/column.jelly` renders ``, and `core/src/main/java/hudson/Util.java` returns `\"\" + s + \"\"`. But the input is not attacker-controlled. In `core/src/main/java/hudson/util/ClockDifference.java`, `toString()` is:\n`String s = Util.getTimeSpanString(abs);`\n`if (diff < 0) s = Messages.ClockDifference_Ahead(s); else s = Messages.ClockDifference_Behind(s);`\n`return s;`\nThe message templates in `core/src/main/resources/hudson/util/Messages.properties` are static: `ClockDifference.Ahead={0} ahead`, `ClockDifference.Behind={0} behind`, `ClockDifference.InSync=In sync`, `ClockDifference.Failed=Failed to check`. The `diff` value itself is numeric-only: `core/src/main/java/hudson/model/Slave.java` computes `new ClockDifference((startTime + endTime) / 2 - remoteTime)` where `startTime`, `endTime`, and `remoteTime` all come from `System.currentTimeMillis()`. `jenkins/model/Jenkins.java` also returns `new ClockDifference(0)`. No node name or arbitrary string is involved. The other caller, `core/src/main/java/hudson/node_monitors/SwapSpaceMonitor.java`, wraps only `Functions.humanReadableByteSize(usage.availableSwapSpace)`, and `core/src/main/java/hudson/Functions.java` formats a `long` into strings like `123.45 MiB`. Therefore no untrusted HTML/JS can reach the sink through the reported paths.", "new_reason:": "" }", "report": "1 ## Vulnerability 1 — `Util.wrapToErrorSpan()` renders unescaped HTML (HIGH) **File:** `core/src/main/java/hudson/Util.java:1478–1481` ```java public static String wrapToErrorSpan(@NonNull String s) { s = "" + s + ""; return s; } ``` **Issue:** The string `s` is directly concatenated into an HTML snippet **with no escaping**. The return value is raw HTML and is passed to Jelly templates via `j:out` (which outputs without additional escaping when given raw HTML). **Call sites:** - `ClockDifference.toHtml()` at `core/src/main/java/hudson/util/ClockDifference.java:89` — `s` comes from `toString()`, which formats a time delta string sourced from server state. If an attacker can control node names or clock difference reporting, this is exploitable. - `SwapSpaceMonitor` at `core/src/main/java/hudson/node_monitors/SwapSpaceMonitor.java:71` — `humanReadableSpace` is formatted from a numeric value, making it lower-risk here. **Template rendering path:** ``` hudson/node_monitors/ClockMonitor/column.jelly:32 → → ClockDifference.toHtml() → Util.wrapToErrorSpan(s) ← unescaped HTML injected ``` `j:out` outputs its value verbatim; combined with `escape-by-default='true'`, `j:out` bypasses the auto-escaping. The HTML produced by `wrapToErrorSpan` is inserted directly into the page. **CWE-079 type:** Stored XSS (if attacker controls the wrapped string's source)" }

{
  "report": "10
## Vulnerability 10 — `editableDescription.jelly` renders `markupFormatter.translate()` via `j:out` (DESIGN RISK)
**File:** `core/src/main/resources/lib/hudson/editableDescription.jelly:52`
```xml
<j:out value="${actualDescription!=null ? app.markupFormatter.translate(actualDescription) : ''}" />
```
`app.markupFormatter.translate()` is called with the stored description and the result is output via `j:out` (raw, no auto-escaping). This is intentional — the markup formatter is supposed to produce safe HTML. But **it is only as safe as the configured `MarkupFormatter` plugin**. If the administrator installs a formatter that doesn't sanitize properly, all descriptions across the system become XSS vectors. The default `EscapedMarkupFormatter` is safe; third-party ones may not be.",
  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "The reported data flow is real, but the sink is misclassified. editableDescription.jelly renders the result of app.markupFormatter.translate(description) as HTML on purpose; in Jenkins, MarkupFormatter is the trusted renderer/sanitizer boundary, not raw user input. MarkupFormatter is explicitly defined as converting markup to HTML, and Jenkins core defaults to EscapedMarkupFormatter, which escapes all HTML metacharacters. The formatter can only be changed by a user with Jenkins.ADMINISTER, so the only way this becomes XSS is if an administrator deliberately installs/configures an unsafe formatter plugin. That would be a vulnerability or insecure policy choice in the selected plugin/trusted extension, not a CWE-079 in Jenkins core. The source paths themselves are permission-gated (for example Run descriptions require UPDATE, View/Node descriptions require CONFIGURE), so this is not an anonymous or accidental injection path. There is no shallow or unreachable-flow issue here; the false positive comes from treating trusted formatter output as an unsafe sink in core.",
  "FP_Reason": ["B1"],
  "evidence": "Sink in core: core/src/main/resources/lib/hudson/editableDescription.jelly:52 renders <j:out value=\"${actualDescription!=null ? app.markupFormatter.translate(actualDescription) : ''}\" />. Formatter contract: core/src/main/java/hudson/markup/MarkupFormatter.java:55-59 says it converts markup to HTML, and lines 90-99 say the implementation writes formatted HTML to the output. Safe default: core/src/main/java/jenkins/model/Jenkins.java:1694-1696 returns new EscapedMarkupFormatter() when no custom formatter is configured. Sanitizer implementation: core/src/main/java/hudson/markup/EscapedMarkupFormatter.java:47-51 calls Util.escape(markup). Admin-only configuration: core/src/main/java/hudson/security/GlobalSecurityConfiguration.java:120-131 checks Jenkins.ADMINISTER before calling setMarkupFormatter(...). Source permission gates: core/src/main/java/hudson/model/Run.java:2369-2381 requires UPDATE for build descriptions; core/src/main/java/hudson/model/View.java:705-710 requires CONFIGURE for view descriptions; core/src/main/java/hudson/slaves/SlaveComputer.java:794-799 requires CONFIGURE for node descriptions. Therefore Jenkins core only becomes script-injectable if a trusted administrator replaces the safe formatter with an unsafe plugin implementation.",
  "new_reason:": ""
}"
}
